California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
James A. Reuben 
Reuben, Quint and Valkevich 
595 Market street, suite 1330 
San Francisco, 
CA 
94105 
Dear Mr. Reuben: 
October 
21, 1988 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-88-369 
You have requested advice regarding your campaign disclosure 
requirements under the Political Reform Act (the "Act").l1 
QUESTION 
May a recipient committee receive as a contribution the 
proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who made 
payments to the fundraiser if the contributors to the fundraiser 
did not know or have reason to know that all or part of their 
payments would be used for political purposes? 
CONCLUSION 
A recipient committee may receive as a contribution the 
proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who 
contributed to the fundraiser if the contributors to the 
fundraiser did not know or have reason to know that all or part of 
their payments would be used for political purposes. 
FACTS 
The San Franciscans for the USS Missouri Yes on Proposition S 
Committee (the "Proposition S Committee"), for which you serve as 
the treasurer, received a contribution of $68,927.28 from the San 
Franciscans for the Homeporting of the USS Missouri (the 
"Homeporting Committee"). 
The Homeporting Committee held a 
fundraising event to launch a media campaign to generate awareness 
and support for homeporting the USS Missouri in San Francisco 
before Proposition S was proposed as and became a ballot measure. 
lIGovernment Code sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations 
Section 18000, et seq. 
All references to regulations are to Title 
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 


James A. Reuben 
Page 2 
There was no representation to any person who contributed to the 
fundraiser sponsored by the Homeporting Committee that any 
contribution would be used to support or to oppose a ballot 
measure. 
ANALYSIS 
The issue you raise is the detail of your disclosure 
obligation as treasurer for the Proposition S committee. 
Specifically, you want to know if the Proposition S committee must 
identify each person who contributed $100 or more to a fundraiser 
which was held by the Homeporting Committee prior to the formation 
of your committee and before there was a ballot question. 
A contribution is a payment which has been made by the donor 
for political purposes. 
(Section 82015.) 
A payment is made for 
political purposes if it is for the purpose of influencing or 
attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against 
the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the 
qualification or passage of any measure. 
(Regulation 18215, copy 
enclosed. ) 
Donations received by an organization, like the Homeporting 
Committee, which is not formed or existing primarily for political 
purposes are "contributions" for purposes of the Act only when, at 
the time of making the payment the donor "knows or has reason to 
know that the payment or funds with which the payment will be 
commingled will be used to make contributions ... expenditures." 
(Regulation 18215(c).) 
In your letter, you indicate that the 
Homeporting Committee did not make any representations to 
potential contributors that they were making payments for the 
political purposes of supporting or opposing a ballot measure or 
any other ballot question. 
Based on this information, we conclude 
that the persons who contributed to the fundraiser did not intend 
to make payments for the purpose of attempting to influence voters 
for or against the qualification or passage of Proposition S. 
Therefore, the Proposition S Committee may receive the 
proceeds of the fundraiser as a contribution from the Homeporting 
Committee without being required to disclose the identity of each 
person who contributed $100 or more to the fundraiser held by the 
Homeporting Committee. 
The Homeporting Committee should be listed 
as the source of the contribution of $68,927.28. 
If the Homeporting Committee is not a recipient committee 
pursuant to Section 82013(a) which has obtained an identification 
number from the Secretary of State, as we understand is the case, 
the Proposition S Committee is required to notify the Homeporting 
Committee that it may be required to file campaign reports. 
(Section 84105.) 
The notice is required to be sent within two 
weeks of receiving the contribution, so if your Committee has not 
already sent a notice to the Homeporting Committee it should do so 
promptly. 


James A. Reuben 
Page 3 
If you have further questions, you may contact me at (916) 
322-5662. 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel 
By: 
Bruce W. Robeck 
Political Reform Consultant

On August 4, 1988, a statement of Organization (Recipient 
Committee) was filed for the San Franciscans for the USS Missouri 
Yes on Proposition S Committee. 
I am the Treasurer of that 
organization. 
The Statement of Organization (Recipient Committee) 
was amended by a filing on August 25, 1988. 
On August 10, 1988, 
the San Franciscans for the USS Missouri Yes on Proposition S 
Committee ("Yes on S") received its first contribution in an 
amount of sixty eight thousand nine hundred twenty seven dollars 
and twenty eight cents ($68,927.28) from San Franciscans for the 
Homeporting of the USS Missouri. 
In an effort to make certain 
that all of the Yes on S Committee filings are proper, I spoke 
with Bruce Robeck at the Fair political Practices commission on 
September 1, 1988. 
I was informed that any money collected from 
individuals prior to the organization of the Yes on S without any 
representation or understanding on the part of the donors that 
monies were being collected in connection with the ballot measure 
or candidate would not be subject to any reporting requirement of 
the Fair Political Practices commission. 
I was further informed 
that issues regarding representations to individual donors is a 
factual good faith question. 
In view of that information, I have taken the opportunity to 
inquire into and investigate the source of the initial 
contribution. 
Those monies were collected at a fundraiser held at 
the Fairmont Hotel on Wednesday, June 15, 1988. 
At the time of 
the fundraiser, none of the organizers nor any of the donors had 
any idea as to whether or not USS Missouri issues would generate 
any ballot measure. 
For 
information, I 
enclosed 
ies 
of all of the relevant documentation including the invitation 
card sent out to a sUbstantial number of individuals supporting 
8000\301075 


Diane M. Griffiths 
Page 2 
September 20, 1988 
the homeporting of the USS Missouri. 
Neither the invitation, the 
RSVP card, or any other of the available documentation reflects, 
in any way, any reference to a future ballot measure. 
The 
individual organizers of the fundraiser have expressly related 
that at the time of the raising of monies, they had no information 
or indication that there would be a necessity for ballot 
measures. 
Under these circumstances, I have come to the conclusion that 
the Yes on S Committee can accept the sixty eight thousand nine 
hundred twenty seven dollars and twenty eight cents ($68,927.28) 
donation as received from the San Franciscans for the Homeporting 
of the USS Missouri. 
If you deem otherwise or require any other 
information or documentation other than as transmitted with this 
correspondence, please let me know. 
I appreciate your review.

THE ECONOMIC BENEFITS OF HOMEPORTING 
THE U.S.S. MISSOURI IN SAN FRANCISCO 
• After the arrival 
the U.SS Missouri. the Bay Area 
II house 17 Naval installations with 
a total annual payroil of $1.3 billion per year. 
• The U.S.s. Missouri Flotilla will bring 5,500 Navy personnel. with an annual payroll of $110 million 
to be spent in 
Francisco. 
• $58 million annually in ship building and repair contracts. 
• $25.6 million spent annually in fuel and suppiies. 
• An estimated 4,000 new direct and indirect jobs in San Francisco. 
• The U.s.s. Missouri attracted 10,000 tourists a day during her last stay the week of July 4th, 
1987. 
• A $33 million housing package for the construction and refurbishing of homes throughout 
the Bay Area, including the construction of 1,500 to 3,000 homes in San Francisco.

On August 4, 1988, a Statement of Organization (Recipient 
Committee) was filed for the San Franciscans for the USS Missouri 
Yes on Proposition S Committee. 
I am the Treasurer of that 
organization. 
The Statement of Organization (Recipient Committee) 
was amended by a filing on August 25, 1988. 
On August 10, 1988, 
the San Franciscans for the USS Missouri Yes on Proposition S 
Committee ("Yes on S") received its first contribution in an 
amount of sixty eight thousand nine hundred twenty seven dollars 
and twenty eight cents ($68,927.28) from San Franciscans for the 
Homeporting of the USS Missouri. 
In an effort to make certain 
that all of the Yes on S Committee filings are proper, I spoke 
with Bruce Robeck at the Fair Political Practices Commission on 
September 1, 1988. 
I was informed that any money collected from 
individuals prior to the organization of the Yes on S without any 
representation or understanding on the part of the donors that 
monies were being collected in connection with the ballot measure 
or candidate would not be subject to any reporting requirement of 
the Fair Political Practices commission. 
I was further informed 
that issues regarding representations to individual donors is a 
factual good faith question. 
In view of that information, I have taken the opportunity to 
inquire into and investigate the source of the initial 
contribution. 
Those monies were collected at a fundraiser held at 
the Fairmont Hotel on Wednesday, June 15, 1988. 
At the time of 
the fundraiser, none of the organizers nor any of the donors had 
any idea as to whether or not USS Missouri issues would generate 
any ballot measure. 
For your information, I have enclosed copies 
of all of the relevant documentation including the invitation 
card sent out to a sUbstantial number of individuals supporting 
8000\301075 


Diane M. Griffiths 
Page 2 
September 20, 1988 
the homeporting of the USS Missouri. 
Neither the invitation, the 
RSVP card, or any other of the available documentation reflects, 
in any way, any reference to a future ballot measure. 
The 
individual organizers of the fundraiser have expressly related 
that at the time of the raising of monies, they had no information 
or indication that there would be a necessity for ballot 
measures. 
Under these circumstances, I have come to the conclusion that 
the Yes on S committee can accept the sixty eight thousand nine 
hundred twenty seven dollars and twenty eight cents ($68,927.28) 
donation as received from the San Franciscans for the Homeporting 
of the USS Missouri. 
If you deem otherwise or require any other 
information or documentation other than as transmitted with this 
correspondence, please let me know. 
I appreciate your review.

sections.question:
May a recipient committee receive as a contribution the 
proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who made 
payments to the fundraiser if the contributors to the fundraiser 
did not know or have reason to know that all or part of their 
payments would be used for political purposes?
sections.conclusion:
A recipient committee may receive as a contribution the 
proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who 
contributed to the fundraiser if the contributors to the 
fundraiser did not know or have reason to know that all or part of 
their payments would be used for political purposes.
sections.facts:
The San Franciscans for the USS Missouri Yes on Proposition S 
Committee (the "Proposition S Committee"), for which you serve as 
the treasurer, received a contribution of $68,927.28 from the San 
Franciscans for the Homeporting of the USS Missouri (the 
"Homeporting Committee"). 
The Homeporting Committee held a 
fundraising event to launch a media campaign to generate awareness 
and support for homeporting the USS Missouri in San Francisco 
before Proposition S was proposed as and became a ballot measure. 
l, et seq. 
All references to regulations are to Title 
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 
 • (916) 322-5660 


James A. Reuben  
There was no representation to any person who contributed to the 
fundraiser sponsored by the Homeporting Committee that any 
contribution would be used to support or to oppose a ballot 
measure.
sections.analysis:
The issue you raise is the detail of your disclosure 
obligation as treasurer for the Proposition S committee. 
Specifically, you want to know if the Proposition S committee must 
identify each person who contributed $100 or more to a fundraiser 
which was held by the Homeporting Committee prior to the formation 
of your committee and before there was a ballot question. 
A contribution is a payment which has been made by the donor 
for political purposes. 
(Section 82015.) 
A payment is made for 
political purposes if it is for the purpose of influencing or 
attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against 
the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the 
qualification or passage of any measure. 
(Regulation 18215, copy 
enclosed. ) 
Donations received by an organization, like the Homeporting 
Committee, which is not formed or existing primarily for political 
purposes are "contributions" for purposes of the Act only when, at 
the time of making the payment the donor "knows or has reason to 
know that the payment or funds with which the payment will be 
commingled will be used to make contributions ... expenditures." 
(Regulation 18215(c).) 
In your letter, you indicate that the 
Homeporting Committee did not make any representations to 
potential contributors that they were making payments for the 
political purposes of supporting or opposing a ballot measure or 
any other ballot question. 
Based on this information, we conclude 
that the persons who contributed to the fundraiser did not intend 
to make payments for the purpose of attempting to influence voters 
for or against the qualification or passage of Proposition S. 
Therefore, the Proposition S Committee may receive the 
proceeds of the fundraiser as a contribution from the Homeporting 
Committee without being required to disclose the identity of each 
person who contributed $100 or more to the fundraiser held by the 
Homeporting Committee. 
The Homeporting Committee should be listed 
as the source of the contribution of $68,927.28. 
If the Homeporting Committee is not a recipient committee 
pursuant to Section 82013(a) which has obtained an identification 
number from the Secretary of State, as we understand is the case, 
the Proposition S Committee is required to notify the Homeporting 
Committee that it may be required to file campaign reports. 
(Section 84105.) 
The notice is required to be sent within two 
weeks of receiving the contribution, so if your Committee has not 
already sent a notice to the Homeporting Committee it should do so 
promptly. 


James A. Reuben

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May a recipient committee receive as a contribution the 
proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who made 
payments to the fundraiser if the contributors to the fundraiser 
did not know or have reason to know that all or part of their 
payments would be used for political purposes?

CONCLUSION: A recipient committee may receive as a contribution the 
proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who 
contributed to the fundraiser if the contributors to the 
fundraiser did not know or have reason to know that all or part of 
their payments would be used for political purposes.

Reuben Reuben, Quint and Valkevich 595 Market street, suite 1330 San Francisco, CA 94105 Dear Mr. Reuben: October 21, 1988 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-88-369 You have requested advice regarding your campaign disclosure requirements under the Political Reform Act (the "Act").l1 QUESTION May a recipient committee receive as a contribution the proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who made payments to the fundraiser if the contributors to the fundraiser did not know or have reason to know that all or part of their payments would be used for political purposes? CONCLUSION A recipient committee may receive as a contribution the proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who contributed to the fundraiser if the contributors to the fundraiser did not know or have reason to know that all or part of their payments would be used for political purposes. FACTS The San Franciscans for the USS Missouri Yes on Proposition S Committee (the "Proposition S Committee"), for which you serve as the treasurer, received a contribution of $68,927.28 from the San Franciscans for the Homeporting of the USS Missouri (the "Homeporting Committee"). The Homeporting Committee held a fundraising event to launch a media campaign to generate awareness and support for homeporting the USS Missouri in San Francisco before Proposition S was proposed as and became a ballot measure. lIGovernment Code sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 James A. Reuben Page 2 There was no representation to any person who contributed to the fundraiser sponsored by the Homeporting Committee that any contribution would be used to support or to oppose a ballot measure.
